focus: Resolve seam continuity with minimal edits.
next_head_excerpt:
# Chapter 2
The museum gave school groups a bright education room with wipe-clean tables and a sink for paint cups, but at five thirty the fluorescent lights flattened everybody into the same bad color. Marisol sat alone at the table closest to the door, legal pad squared with the table edge, recorder centered on top of her residency folder like it might behave better under official paper. Through the cinderblock wall she heard a cart wheel squeal every eight seconds as a guard pushed stanchions back into storage. The room smelled less like art than cafeteria bleach and the sour sugar of old apple juice.

She had written three lines at the top of the page before he came in.

Objective: test credibility.

No myth language in the questions.

Get one concrete, verifiable claim.

When the orderly opened the door, Salvador did not look at her first. He stared at the sink like he was trying to remember what water was called, then held up his own right hand and flexed each finger, checking it like a tool he no longer trusted. He was taller than any chair let him be, even folded forward in the transport chair, long knees like bent coat hangers under a museum blanket. The skin under his eyes was the yellow of nicotine stains. He carried his pain in stages: first the jaw set, then the breath, and then, if it got bad enough, a tiny dry click in his throat before he swallowed.

"You picked the student room," he said while the orderly parked him. "I like your optimism. Children paint better lies than adults."

"It was available," Marisol said.

"So was heaven."

The orderly gave Marisol a quick apology with his eyebrows, checked the morphine log clipped to the back of the chair, and left. The door shut with a soft seal. Salvador tugged the blanket off his legs and winced when the wo...
